data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-12T01:37:57.174Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is about a known breaking change in EF Core. Let me recall what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, synchronous operations were never natively supported — they were implemented as sync-over-async internally. In EF Core 10 (.NET 10), there was an opt-in mechanism to allow this pattern. In .NET 11 (EF Core 11), this sync-over-async support is being removed entirely.


Given the current date is May 2026, .NET 11 would still be in preview since it's not released until November 2026. The Cosmos DB provider has historically only supported async operations natively, and while EF Core 9 allowed you to opt into sync-over-async behavior with an exception thrown by default, the trajectory suggests this compatibility layer is being phased out entirely in the newer versions.

So the breaking change is that .NET 11 / EF Core 11 removes sync-over-async support completely for the Cosmos DB provider. Any synchronous LINQ methods like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, or `SaveChanges()` will throw `InvalidOperationException` because the provider only natively supports async operations. The solution is straightforward: convert all those calls to their async counterparts like `ToListAsync()`, `FirstAsync()`, `CountAsync()`, and `SaveChangesAsync()`.
